title: The case for boring infrastructure
summary: Argue that society should invest more attention and funding in unglamorous but essential systems such as water pipes, sewers, and electricity grids.
prompt: Write an argumentative essay (350–500 words) making the case that unglamorous infrastructure — water pipes, sewers, electricity grids, maintenance depots — deserves far more public attention and funding than it typically receives. Refer to at least one real failure or investment (e.g. the 2021 Texas power grid collapse, London's Victorian sewer network, or Japan's infrastructure inspection programmes). Use a cleft sentence, a concession, and at least one passive construction. Take a clear position.
hintStarters: In February 2021, more than four million Texas households lost power during a winter storm…
What the Texas blackout exposed was not a shortage of generation capacity but a failure to…
Granted, spending on visible infrastructure — a new stadium, a high-speed railway — wins votes in a way that…
The pipes beneath a city are its immune system: invisible until they…

hintVocab.meaningVi: lão hoá / trở nên quá cũ
trách nhiệm pháp lý dài hạn
khai thác cạn kiệt tài sản
vô hình cho đến khi hỏng

sampleText:
In February 2021, more than four million Texas households lost power during a winter storm that lasted less than a week. Pipes burst inside homes. Hospitals ran on generators. People died. The event was widely reported as a natural disaster, but what the Texas blackout exposed was not unusual weather — it was decades of deferred maintenance on a grid that had never been properly winterised. The failure was not dramatic in origin; it was the cumulative result of small decisions to spend on something else.

This is the central paradox of modern infrastructure: the systems that matter most are the ones most easily ignored. A new concert hall is photographed at the ribbon-cutting; a replaced water main is noted only in a budget line. Politicians who fund sewers do not attract admiring headlines. Voters who benefit from a grid that does not fail have no visible evidence of the investment working — reliability is, by definition, the absence of events. As a result, maintenance is perpetually deferred in favour of projects that can be seen.

Granted, there are legitimate reasons to prioritise visible investment. A new metro line creates jobs, reduces commuting times, and signals economic ambition. These are genuine public goods, not mere vanity. The argument is not that glamorous projects should be cancelled but that the invisible work beneath them — the cables, the drainage, the load-bearing foundations — is systematically underfunded by comparison.

What makes this especially perverse is that deferred maintenance is never free. Every year that a bridge inspection is postponed, every winter that pipework is left uninsulated, the eventual bill grows. London's Victorian sewers, built in the 1860s under Joseph Bazalgette, are still in use today and still being patched; the cost of replacing them properly is now estimated in the tens of billions. Japan, by contrast, has invested heavily in regular infrastructure inspection programmes since the 1990s, and the country is measurably more resilient to earthquakes and storms as a result. The boring work, done consistently, insulates entire populations against crisis.

Societies tend to notice infrastructure only when it fails, at which point the cost — in money, in lives, and in the political capital required to repair it — is far higher than early investment would have been. The unglamorous is not the unimportant. A city whose sewers hold is a city whose people can get on with their lives. That deserves a ribbon-cutting of its own.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: 'What' cleft to pinpoint the real cause
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'what the Texas blackout exposed was not unusual weather — it was decades of deferred maintenance' dùng cấu trúc 'what X exposed was Y' để đẩy lý do thực sự (bảo trì bị trì hoãn) ra cuối câu, nơi nó nhận được nhấn mạnh lớn nhất và tạo ra hiệu ứng 'bật ngược' với kỳ vọng của người đọc.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'what the Texas blackout exposed was not unusual weather — it was decades of deferred maintenance' uses a what-cleft to delay the true cause until the end, creating a contrast with the expected explanation and maximising the rhetorical impact.

sampleGrammarNotes.title: Passive to separate system from blame
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyVi: 'maintenance is perpetually deferred in favour of projects that can be seen' dùng bị động để tránh chỉ đích danh ai chịu trách nhiệm, ám chỉ rằng đây là mô hình hệ thống chứ không phải lỗi của một cá nhân cụ thể — một kỹ thuật thường thấy trong văn phân tích chính sách ở trình độ B2 trở lên.
sampleGrammarNotes.bodyEn: 'maintenance is perpetually deferred' uses a passive to describe a systemic pattern without attributing blame to a single actor — a common move in policy writing that signals analytical detachment.

sampleTranslationVi:
Vào tháng Hai năm 2021, hơn bốn triệu hộ gia đình ở Texas mất điện trong một cơn bão mùa đông kéo dài chưa đầy một tuần. Đường ống vỡ bên trong nhà. Bệnh viện chạy bằng máy phát điện. Người chết. Sự kiện này được đưa tin rộng rãi như một thảm họa tự nhiên, nhưng điều mà vụ mất điện Texas phơi bày không phải là thời tiết bất thường — mà là nhiều thập kỷ bảo trì bị trì hoãn trên một lưới điện chưa bao giờ được bảo vệ đúng cách cho mùa đông. Sự cố này không có nguồn gốc kịch tính; đó là kết quả tích lũy của những quyết định nhỏ để chi tiêu cho thứ khác.

Đây là nghịch lý trung tâm của cơ sở hạ tầng hiện đại: những hệ thống quan trọng nhất là những hệ thống dễ bị bỏ qua nhất. Một nhà hát hòa nhạc mới được chụp ảnh lúc cắt băng khánh thành; một đường ống nước được thay thế chỉ được ghi nhận trong một dòng ngân sách. Các chính trị gia tài trợ cho hệ thống cống không thu hút tiêu đề khen ngợi. Cử tri hưởng lợi từ một lưới điện không bị hỏng không có bằng chứng hữu hình nào về khoản đầu tư hiệu quả — độ tin cậy, theo định nghĩa, là sự vắng mặt của các sự kiện. Do đó, bảo trì liên tục bị hoãn để ưu tiên các dự án có thể nhìn thấy được.

Công nhận, có những lý do chính đáng để ưu tiên đầu tư có thể nhìn thấy. Một tuyến tàu điện ngầm mới tạo ra việc làm, giảm thời gian đi lại và báo hiệu tham vọng kinh tế. Đây là những hàng hóa công cộng thực sự, không chỉ là phù phiếm. Lập luận không phải là các dự án hào nhoáng nên bị hủy bỏ mà là công việc vô hình bên dưới chúng — cáp điện, hệ thống thoát nước, nền móng chịu lực — bị thiếu vốn một cách có hệ thống khi so sánh.

Điều khiến điều này đặc biệt nghịch lý là bảo trì bị trì hoãn không bao giờ miễn phí. Mỗi năm mà một cuộc kiểm tra cầu bị hoãn lại, mỗi mùa đông mà hệ thống ống dẫn bị để không được cách nhiệt, hóa đơn cuối cùng ngày càng tăng. Hệ thống cống Victorian của London, được xây dựng vào những năm 1860 dưới thời Joseph Bazalgette, vẫn đang được sử dụng ngày nay và vẫn đang được vá; chi phí thay thế chúng đúng cách hiện được ước tính lên tới hàng chục tỷ bảng. Nhật Bản, ngược lại, đã đầu tư mạnh vào các chương trình kiểm tra cơ sở hạ tầng thường xuyên từ những năm 1990, và đất nước này có khả năng phục hồi sau động đất và bão có thể đo lường được. Công việc tẻ nhạt, được thực hiện nhất quán, bảo vệ toàn bộ dân số khỏi khủng hoảng.

Các xã hội thường chú ý đến cơ sở hạ tầng chỉ khi nó hỏng, lúc đó chi phí — về tiền bạc, sinh mạng và vốn chính trị cần thiết để sửa chữa nó — cao hơn nhiều so với đầu tư sớm. Cái tẻ nhạt không phải là cái không quan trọng. Một thành phố mà hệ thống cống thoát nước hoạt động tốt là một thành phố mà người dân có thể tiếp tục cuộc sống của họ. Điều đó xứng đáng được cắt băng khánh thành.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence uses a 'what' cleft to pinpoint the real cause of the Texas disaster?
options: The Texas blackout was caused by unusual weather and poor government response.
what the Texas blackout exposed was not unusual weather — it was decades of deferred maintenance
It was the Texas blackout that exposed unusual weather as the main cause.
What happened in Texas exposed the government's failure to act in time.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What X exposed was Y' delays the key finding (deferred maintenance) to the end, contrasting it with the expected explanation (unusual weather).
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the structure 'what X exposed was not A — it was B'.

options: Maintenance that has been completed ahead of schedule
Maintenance that has been postponed, allowing problems to accumulate
Maintenance that is performed by a separate contractor
Maintenance that is considered too expensive to attempt
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Deferred' means postponed; 'deferred maintenance' is upkeep that is delayed, often repeatedly, causing systems to deteriorate.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'defer' as 'put off until later'.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which passive construction correctly describes a systemic pattern without naming anyone to blame?
options: Politicians defer maintenance in favour of projects that can be seen.
maintenance is perpetually deferred in favour of projects that can be seen
Someone has always deferred maintenance for projects that people can see.
Maintenance, which is deferred, is favoured by projects that can be seen.
mcQuestions.explanation: The passive 'is perpetually deferred' removes the agent (who is doing the deferring), signalling that this is a structural pattern rather than one person's fault.
mcQuestions.hint: Which option removes the actor entirely to describe a systemic trend?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'Granted' as a concession opener?
options: Granted, there are legitimate reasons to prioritise visible investment.
Granted that there are legitimate reasons for prioritise visible investment.
Granted which there are legitimate reasons prioritise visible investment.
Granted, and there are legitimate reasons so prioritise visible investment.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Granted, + clause' is correct. It introduces the opposing argument before the writer pivots back to their own position.
mcQuestions.hint: 'Granted,' is followed immediately by a complete clause with no extra conjunction.

mcQuestions.prompt: What is the essay's main argument about invisible infrastructure?
options: Governments should stop building glamorous projects entirely.
Visible investment always produces worse outcomes than invisible maintenance.
Unglamorous maintenance is systematically underfunded compared to its importance.
Infrastructure problems are too expensive to be solved by any government.
mcQuestions.explanation: The essay argues that essential but invisible systems are underfunded relative to their importance, not that visible projects are worthless.
mcQuestions.hint: The essay does not call for abolishing new projects — it calls for a better balance.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses 'insulate against' in a figurative sense?
options: The boring work, done consistently, insulates entire populations against crisis.
The boring work, done consistently, insulates entire populations from of crisis.
Insulating entire populations, the boring work does consistently against crisis.
Entire populations insulate the boring work against crisis consistently.
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Insulate against' is the correct collocation when used figuratively to mean 'protect from'. No preposition switch or inversion is needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Check the preposition: 'insulate against' not 'insulate from of'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'resilience' mean in the context of infrastructure?
options: The ability to attract international investment
The visual appeal of a public building
The capacity to recover quickly from disruption or damage
The legal framework governing public procurement
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Resilience' in infrastructure refers to a system's ability to withstand and recover from shocks such as storms, earthquakes, or load spikes.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of 'resilience' as 'bouncing back' from difficulty.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which phrase means 'the total effect of many small individual decisions'?
options: a cascade failure
the cumulative result of small decisions
capital expenditure
a long-term liability
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Cumulative result' refers to an effect that builds up over time from many individual inputs, each small but collectively significant.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for the adjective that means 'adding up over time'.

mcQuestions.prompt: What contrast does the essay draw between London and Japan?
options: London has a newer sewer system; Japan's is older and in worse condition.
London's Victorian sewers are still being patched expensively; Japan invested early and became more resilient.
Japan and London both deferred maintenance and both paid heavily for it.
London invested in inspection programmes; Japan chose to build new infrastructure instead.
mcQuestions.explanation: London's Victorian sewers are an example of the cost of deferral; Japan's inspection programmes are the positive counterexample.
mcQuestions.hint: One city represents the problem; the other represents the solution.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which transition best signals a pivot from concession back to the main argument?
options: In conclusion,
For example,
What makes this especially perverse is that
Moreover,
mcQuestions.explanation: 'What makes this especially perverse is that' is a cleft-style pivot that re-engages the main argument by introducing a further complication after the concession.
mcQuestions.hint: Look for a phrase that reintroduces the writer's concern after acknowledging the other side.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence best avoids AI-style filler and opens with concrete evidence?
options: In today's modern world, infrastructure is extremely important to all societies.
In February 2021, more than four million Texas households lost power during a winter storm that lasted less than a week.
It is important to note that infrastructure failures can have very serious consequences.
Throughout history, societies have always needed to invest in their infrastructure systems.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B opens with a specific date, place, number, and event — no generic framing needed.
mcQuestions.hint: Concrete date + place + statistic beats any generic opener.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which word means 'to gradually become worse in quality or condition'?
options: to insulate
to defer
to deteriorate
to procure
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Deteriorate' means to decline progressively in quality, strength, or condition — exactly what happens to unmaintained infrastructure.
mcQuestions.hint: Which word contains the idea of gradual decline?

mcQuestions.prompt: Which sentence correctly uses a non-defining relative clause to add extra information?
options: London's Victorian sewers which were built in the 1860s are still in use today.
London's Victorian sewers, built in the 1860s under Joseph Bazalgette, are still in use today and still being patched.
London's Victorian sewers, that were built in the 1860s, are still in use today.
London's Victorian sewers built in the 1860s that are still in use today.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option B uses a participial phrase ('built in the 1860s under Joseph Bazalgette') as a non-defining supplement, which is both accurate and concise at B2 level.
mcQuestions.hint: A participial phrase set off by commas avoids the awkward 'which' construction.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does the essay mean by 'reliability is, by definition, the absence of events'?
options: Reliable infrastructure never needs to be tested.
When infrastructure works perfectly, there are no newsworthy incidents, so the investment goes unnoticed.
Reliability means that all events on the grid are carefully logged.
Reliable systems do not require any maintenance because they never fail.
mcQuestions.explanation: The writer is pointing out that successful maintenance creates no visible proof — nothing goes wrong, so there is nothing to report or praise.
mcQuestions.hint: Think about when infrastructure makes the news — only when it breaks.

mcQuestions.prompt: Which option maintains a consistent B2 formal register?
options: Tbh fixing pipes is boring but someone has to do it lol.
Like, governments should maybe spend more on boring stuff?
The unglamorous is not the unimportant. A city whose sewers hold is a city whose people can get on with their lives.
Old pipes = bad. Fix them!!! It's not rocket science people.
mcQuestions.explanation: Option C uses precise vocabulary, an aphoristic structure, and a controlled tone appropriate for B2 analytical writing.
mcQuestions.hint: Avoid slang, abbreviations, rhetorical exclamations, and informal equations.

mcQuestions.prompt: What does 'political capital' mean in this essay?
options: The city that hosts a country's parliament
The money raised through political fundraising campaigns
The trust, goodwill, and influence a politician has to spend on difficult decisions
The financial cost of running an election campaign
mcQuestions.explanation: 'Political capital' is a metaphor for the goodwill and public trust a leader has available — like money, it can be spent on controversial decisions.
mcQuestions.hint: Think of it as a resource a politician holds and can choose to spend.

criticalThinkingQuestion: Think of a piece of infrastructure you rely on every day — a water pipe, a road, a power line. When did you last think about how it works or who maintains it? What would it take to make the maintenance of essential systems a genuinely popular political cause?
